02 Layout Mental Models — Four Universes

← Back to lessons

CSS has four layout universes. Block, inline, flex, and grid each follow different physics. Properties that work in one universe may do nothing in another. The #1 layout mistake is treating them as one system.

The Mental Model: Universes with Different Physics

When you set display: flex on an element, you're not just "turning on flexbox." You're changing which universe its children live in. And each universe has fundamentally different rules:

UniverseDimensionCore IdeaTailwind
BlockVerticalElements stack top-to-bottom. Each takes full width.block
InlineHorizontalElements flow left-to-right like text. Width/height ignored.inline
FlexOne axisChildren align along a main axis. Space distribution.flex
GridTwo axesChildren placed in rows AND columns simultaneously.grid

The key insight: display doesn't style the element itself — it changes the layout rules for that element's children. When you write display: flex, you're saying "my children now live in the flex universe." The element itself still participates in whatever its parent's layout mode is.

The Box Model: Every Element Is a Box

Before layout modes, you need the box model. Every element is a nested set of rectangles:

┌──────────────────────── margin ────────────────────────┐
│  ┌──────────────────── border ───────────────────────┐  │
│  │  ┌──────────────── padding ─────────────────────┐  │  │
│  │  │                                               │  │  │
│  │  │              content area                     │  │  │
│  │  │         (your text, images, etc.)             │  │  │
│  │  │                                               │  │  │
│  │  └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

The box-sizing gotcha

content-box (default, confusing)
.box {
  width: 300px;
  padding: 20px;
  border: 2px solid;
}
/* Actual width on screen:
   300 + 20*2 + 2*2 = 344px
   The 300px only sizes the CONTENT.
   Padding and border add on top. */
border-box (what you actually want)
.box {
  box-sizing: border-box;
  width: 300px;
  padding: 20px;
  border: 2px solid;
}
/* Actual width on screen:
   300px total.
   Content shrinks to fit.
   This is why every reset includes
   *, *::before, *::after {
     box-sizing: border-box;
   } */

Tailwind connection. Tailwind's preflight (reset) sets box-sizing: border-box on everything. That's why w-[300px] p-5 border-2 gives you exactly 300px wide. Without border-box, it'd be 344px and nothing would line up.

Block Flow: The Default Universe

Block-level elements (div, p, h1-h6, section) follow these rules:

  • Each element takes the full available width of its parent
  • Elements stack vertically, one below the other
  • width and height work as expected
  • Vertical margins collapse (the tricky part)

Margin collapsing — the hidden gotcha

/* These two paragraphs have 20px between them, NOT 40px */
p { margin-bottom: 20px; }
p { margin-top: 20px; }
/* Adjacent vertical margins collapse to the larger value.
   This only happens in block flow. Flex and grid don't collapse. */

Why Tailwind uses padding for spacing. Margin collapsing is unpredictable. That's one reason Tailwind's space-y-4 utility uses margin-top on all but the first child — and why many design systems prefer gap in flex/grid (where margins don't collapse) over margin-based spacing.

Inline Flow: The Text Universe

Inline elements (span, a, strong, em) follow text rules:

  • They flow left-to-right, wrapping like words in a paragraph
  • width and height are ignored
  • Vertical padding and margin don't push other elements away
  • They sit on the text baseline

The inline trap. New developers constantly try to set width or height on <span> or <a> elements and wonder why nothing happens. These are inline elements — they don't respond to box dimensions. You need display: inline-block or display: block first.

inline-block: the hybrid

/* inline-block = inline flow + block box model */
.tag {
  display: inline-block;   /* flows like text, but... */
  width: 80px;             /* this works now! */
  height: 32px;            /* this too! */
  padding: 4px 12px;       /* vertical padding pushes things */
}
/* Tailwind: inline-block */

Formatting Contexts: The Invisible Boundaries

A Block Formatting Context (BFC) is an invisible boundary that contains floats, prevents margin collapse with the outside, and creates a self-contained layout region.

Things that create a new BFC:

  • display: flow-root — the explicit way (best)
  • overflow: hidden — the old hack (has side effects)
  • display: flex or display: grid — automatically creates one
  • Absolutely or fixed positioned elements
  • display: inline-block
/* Old way to "clear" floats and contain margins */
.container { overflow: hidden; }  /* creates BFC but clips content */

/* Modern way */
.container { display: flow-root; }  /* creates BFC, no side effects */

Why this matters for design systems. When building reusable components, each component should create its own formatting context. This prevents margin collapse from leaking styles between components. Use display: flow-root, or just use flex/grid (which create BFCs automatically).

Stacking Contexts: The Z-Axis

Stacking contexts control which elements appear in front of others. Think of them as layers in Photoshop — each context is its own layer group.

A new stacking context is created by:

  • position: relative/absolute/fixed with a z-index value
  • opacity less than 1
  • transform, filter, backdrop-filter
  • isolation: isolate — the clean way to create one
/* The z-index mystery: why z-index: 9999 doesn't work */
.modal-overlay {
  position: fixed;
  z-index: 9999;  /* Useless if the parent creates a stacking context */
}

/* If a parent has transform: translateX(0) or opacity: 0.99,
   it creates a new stacking context. Your z-index: 9999 only
   competes WITHIN that context, not with elements outside it. */

/* The fix: use isolation: isolate on intentional containers */
.app-layout { isolation: isolate; }
/* Tailwind: isolate */

z-index only works within the same stacking context. If your modal is inside a transform-ed container, its z-index fights with siblings in that container, not the rest of the page. This is the #1 source of "why is my modal behind the overlay" bugs.

The Decision Tree: Which Layout Mode?

Is the layout one-dimensional (row OR column)?
├── YES → Use Flexbox
│   └── Is it a simple row of items? → flex
│   └── Is it a nav bar? → flex
│   └── Is it centering something? → flex + align/justify
│
└── NO → Is it two-dimensional (rows AND columns)?
    ├── YES → Use Grid
    │   └── Is it a page layout with sidebar? → grid
    │   └── Is it a card grid? → grid
    │   └── Do you need items to span rows AND columns? → grid
    │
    └── Is it just normal document flow?
        └── YES → Use Block (the default)
            └── Paragraphs, headings, stacked sections → block

The real answer: Use flex for distributing items along one axis. Use grid for placing items in two dimensions. Use both in the same project. They're not competing — they're complementary. A grid layout's cells can contain flex containers, and vice versa.

Check Your Understanding

1. You set width: 200px on a <span> and nothing happens. Why?

<span> is an inline element. Inline elements ignore width and height. Change it to display: inline-block or display: block first.

2. Two adjacent <div>s each have margin: 20px 0. How much space is between them?

20px, not 40px. Vertical margins in block flow collapse to the larger value. If one had 30px and the other 20px, the gap would be 30px.

3. Does margin collapsing happen inside a flex container?

No. Margin collapsing only happens in block formatting context. Flex and grid containers create new formatting contexts where margins don't collapse.

4. What does display: flex change about the element's CHILDREN?

It puts the children into the flex layout universe. They now respond to flex properties (justify-content, align-items, flex-grow, etc.) and no longer follow block flow rules.

5. Your modal has z-index: 9999 but appears behind a dropdown. What's likely happening?

The modal is inside a parent element that creates a stacking context (via transform, opacity, filter, etc.). The z-index only competes within that context, not with elements outside it. The fix: move the modal out of the stacking context, or use isolation: isolate strategically.

Exercise: Identify the Layout Universe

Open the DevTools on this platform's homepage and identify:

  1. Find 3 elements using block flow (normal document stacking)
  2. Find 2 elements using flexbox (check for display: flex)
  3. Find 1 element using grid (check for display: grid)
  4. For each, explain WHY that layout mode was chosen for that context
  5. Bonus: Find an element that creates a new stacking context

Recap

  1. Four layout universes — block, inline, flex, grid — each with different physics
  2. display changes children's universe, not the element itself
  3. box-sizing: border-box makes width mean "total width including padding and border"
  4. Margins collapse in block flow only — not in flex or grid
  5. Inline elements ignore width/height — use inline-block or block
  6. Formatting contexts contain floats and prevent margin collapse leaking
  7. Stacking contexts scope z-index — the #1 source of layering bugs
  8. Flex = one axis, Grid = two axes — they complement, not compete
← Lesson 01: Cascade Lesson 03: Flexbox →
